results.title: ConocoPhillips (NYSE:COP) | Economic Value Added
results.content: Net operating profit after taxes (NOPAT) exhibited a substantial increase from 2021 to 2022, followed by declines in subsequent years. In summary, while initial performance was strong, the period concluded with a substantial decline in economic profit, driven by decreasing NOPAT and increasing invested capital. While economic profit decreased overall during the period, the larger increase in invested capital appears to be a primary driver of the diminishing economic spread ratio in the later years. Conversely, the subsequent declines in the ratio are associated with decreasing economic profit, coupled with a growing invested capital base. The most pronounced decrease in the ratio occurs in 2024 and 2025, despite economic profit remaining positive, suggesting that the growth in invested capital outpaced the ability to generate returns. :   While sales and other operating revenues increased from 2021 to 2022, the subsequent decrease in sales in 2023 and relatively flat sales in 2024 and 2025 occurred concurrently with the decline in economic profit margin.

Chevron reported that adjusted free cash flow rose over 35% year over year excluding asset sales, even as oil prices declined, highlighting the company’s ability to generate strong cash flow across cycles. At the same time, Chevron is improving profitability through structural cost reductions, targeting about $3 billion to $4 billion in savings by 2026, which lowers breakeven oil prices and strengthens returns even if commodity prices stabilize. Free cash flow remains the core driver of value, as Chevron prioritizes dividends and share buybacks, allowing the company to return capital consistently while maintaining balance sheet strength. # Chevron Smashes Production Records, Navigates Price Volatility to Deliver 2025 Earnings Beat In a year marked by a significant "lower for longer" pricing environment and shifting global energy dynamics, Chevron Corporation (NYSE: CVX) reported a resilient fourth-quarter 2025 performance that exceeded analyst expectations on the bottom line. Despite facing a sharp decline in average Brent crude pricesâ€”averaging $69 per barrel in 2025 compared to $81 in 2024â€”the San Ramon-based oil major leveraged its newly expanded portfolio and massive gains in operational efficiency to return a record-setting $27.1 billion to shareholders over the course of the fiscal year. The January 30, 2026, earnings report highlights a pivotal moment for the energy giant, coming just months after the full integration of its $53 billion acquisition of Hess Corporation. As of the end of Q4 2025, Chevron has officially transformed into a production powerhouse, clocking in a worldwide output of 4.045 million barrels of oil equivalent per day (MBOED). This landmark achievement, driven largely by the companyâ€™s dominance in the U.S. Permian Basin and high-margin gains in Guyana, underscores Chevronâ€™s ability to generate significant cash flow even as macroeconomic  ...    Chevron's Q4 2025 adjusted earnings per share (EPS) of $1.52 comfortably beat the consensus estimate of $1.47, a feat the company attributed to aggressive cost-cutting and record-breaking production volumes. Total production rose 21% year-over-year in the final quarter, fueled by the successful integration of Hess assets and a monumental milestone in the Permian Basin. For the first time in its history, Chevron averaged 1 million MBOED in the Permian for the full year 2025, reaching this goal with 10% less capital expenditure than initially projected. This efficiency was mirrored in  ...    The road to these results was not without its obstacles. Throughout 2025, Chevron navigated a complex landscape of  ...    which similarly reported record production of 4.7 million MBOED following its Pioneer Natural Resources acquisition.  ...    This trend is not isolated; the entire U.S. oil sector reached record output levels of 13.5 million b/d in 2025,  ...    In summary, Chevronâ€™s 2025 performance is a masterclass in corporate resilience. By hitting a 1 million barrel per day milestone in the Permian and successfully absorbing Hess Corporation, the company has insulated itself against the very price drops that are currently unsettling the wider market. The Q4 earnings beat serves as a validation of CEO Mike Wirthâ€™s strategy: prioritize capital discipline, focus on the most profitable barrels, and reward shareholders  ...    begins to exert downward pressure on prices. For investors, the takeaway is clear: the energy sector has bifurcated into the "haves" and "have-nots." With its 39-year streak of dividend increases intact and its production levels at an  ...    ---
